title: Cotoneaster henryanus
taxonomy: Cotoneaster henryanus is a species within the family Rosaceae. The family Rosaceae is a large and diverse group of flowering plants commonly known as the rose family. This family also includes other well-known plants such as roses, apples, and pears. Within the Rosaceae family, Cotoneaster henryanus is part of the genus Cotoneaster, a group of shrubs native to Asia. The genus Cotoneaster is further divided into several species, with Cotoneaster henryanus being one of the more popular and ornamental species.
uses: Cotoneaster henryanus is primarily an ornamental plant, but its uses in traditional medicine are limited to certain regions. In some Asian cultures, the plant's berries, leaves, and stems are used to treat various ailments. The berries are said to have antioxidant properties and are used to treat digestive issues. The leaves are used to treat fever and other inflammatory conditions. However, more research is needed to confirm the efficacy and safety of these medicinal uses.

The berries of the Cotoneaster plant are rich in antioxidants, which can help protect the body from oxidative stress and inflammation. parts: Cotoneaster henryanus is a shrub native to China, and while it's primarily known for its ornamental value, research on its medicinal properties is limited. side_effects: Cotoneaster henryanus is a non-toxic plant, but it may cause skin irritation or allergic reactions in some individuals due to the sap or leaves. The plant's berries are toxic to humans and animals if ingested, as they contain a compound called amygdalin, which releases cyanide when broken down. Ingestion of the plant's berries can lead to symptoms such as vomiting, diarrhea, and abdominal pain. However, the severity of the symptoms depends on the amount and frequency of consumption. It's essential to handle the plant with care and avoid ingesting its parts to prevent any adverse effects.
